Maternal bisphenols exposure and thyroid function in children: a systematic review and meta-analysis

evidence_entities.text: Prenatal bisphenol concentrations were statistically significant related to alterations in thyroid hormones in children, exclusively in female offspring, including reduced TSH (β = -0.020, 95% CI: -0.036, -0.005) and increased TT3 levels (β = 0.011, 95% CI: 0.001, 0.021)
exposure to high concentration of bisphenols (>1.5 ug/g creatinine) significantly reduced FT3 levels in children (β = -0.011, 95% CI: -0.020, -0.003)
BPs may cause thyroid hormone disturbances by directly interacting with TH receptors
BPs interfere with the expression of genes related to thyroid function in both FRTL5 cells and zebrafish embryos by modulating the expression of thyroid transcription factors Foxe1, Nkx2-1, and Pax8
BPs also inhibit sodium/iodine transporters, thereby affecting thyroid hormone signaling and action
Prenatal BPs exposure on TSH levels indicated a modest, yet statistically significant reduction in TSH levels in total children (β = -0.013, 95% CI: -0.025, -0.001)
this reduction was pronounced in female offspring (β = -0.020, 95% CI: -0.036, -0.005), and no significant association was observed in male (β = -0.005, 95%CI: -0.024, 0.015)
a significant increase was noted in females (β = 0.011, 95% CI: 0.001, 0.021) for TT3 levels
